content.problemStatement:
You are given a list of integers heightsheightsheights representing the heights of NNN mountain peaks. For each peak, you need to find the height of the next higher peak that comes after it in the list. If there is no higher peak after a given peak, return −1-1−1 for that peak.


Input Format

The first line contains one integer nnn, the size of the list of integers.                                                                                                               - Next line contains nnn integers heights[0],heights[1]...heights[n]heights[0],heights[1]...heights[n]heights[0],heights[1]...heights[n], representing the peaks .


Output Format

For each peak, print the next higher peak that comes after it in the list. If there is no higher peak for a peak than print −1-1−1.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
The next greater element for 1 is 2 and for 2 there is no next greater element so for 2 it will be -1.

Explanation:
For the first 6 there is no element greater than 6 on its right so the peak for the first 6 is -1. For 5 , 6 is the next peak as it is on the right side and is greater than 5. For the next element 3 , 6 is the peak as it is on the right side and is greater than 3, for the last 6 , no peak is there on the right side so -1 is there.

solution.approach:
This problem can be efficiently solved using a stack-based approach, which allows us to traverse the list of peaks once and determine the next higher peak for each element in linear time.

The main idea is to iterate through the list of peaks while maintaining a stack to keep track of the indices of peaks for which we are yet to find a higher peak. As we move through the list, we compare the current peak’s height with the heights of the peaks stored in the stack. If the current peak is higher than the peak at the index stored at the top of the stack, we have found the next higher peak for that index, so we update the result and pop the index from the stack. We continue this process until we’ve processed all peaks. Peaks that never find a higher peak after them will retain the default value of `-1` in the result.
solution.timeComplexity: The solution runs in O(N) time, where N is the number of peaks, since each element is pushed and popped from the stack at most once.
solution.spaceComplexity: The space complexity is O(N) for the stack and the result vector.
